He told me that I would need a car there.
She said that the app raised prices at rush hour.
Marcus, before my trip I asked a few friends how to get around, and everyone said something different!
Ha, that sounds right. What did your New York friend tell you?
She told me that I would barely need a car, because the city was walkable and the subway ran everywhere.
Smart. And the friend in Los Angeles?
He said that I would be lost without a car, and that the urban sprawl went on for miles.
So they told you two opposite things. What did you decide?
One friend told me that the smartest travelers always ask a local. So that is exactly what I am doing right now!
Then here is my local tip: take the subway crosstown, and let the app reroute you around any bottleneck.
She told me that the city was walkable.
He said that I would need a car.
My friend explained that the streets were numbered.
They said that prices went up at rush hour.
My friend told me that the subway ran all night.
A local told me that I could walk everywhere.

Hi Lucimara, it is Sofia. So you asked me how to get around when you visit next month, and here is my honest advice. Downtown is completely walkable, so you will not need a car for most of the trip. The streets follow a grid system, so they are numbered and really easy to find. One warning: if you take a rideshare during rush hour, be careful, because the app uses surge pricing and you will pay a lot more. My real tip is to walk or take the subway crosstown, and if a street is blocked, the app will reroute you around the bottleneck. Oh, and there is a beautiful bike lane along the river if you want to rent a bike for a day. Call me when you land. Bye!

Here is a quick tip for reporting what other people say to you. When you tell a friend what someone said, you usually move the verb one step into the past. If your friend says I live here, you report it as she said that she lived here. Present becomes past. Will becomes would, so I will help you becomes he said that he would help me. And can becomes could, so I can drive becomes she said that she could drive. Two verbs to remember: use say with no person, she said that, but use tell with a person, she told me that. Get those right, and you can report any piece of advice you hear on your trip.
